vulnerability_analysis.vulnerability_type: JSON Injection via Unescaped String Interpolation

root_cause.description: The original implementation used bash heredoc (cat <<EOF...EOF) to construct Discord webhook JSON payloads, with user-controlled variables directly interpolated into the JSON structure without escaping.
root_cause.code_pattern: String variables ($BEAT_NUMBER, $STATUS, $MESSAGE, $commit_msg, etc.) embedded unquoted in JSON heredoc
root_cause.interpolation_context: ${...} and bare $VAR expansion in double-quoted heredoc

attack_vectors.impact: Malformed JSON or injected Discord embed fields redirecting users
Broken JSON structure; Discord message fails silently
JSON parsing error; escapes terminate strings prematurely

attack_vectors.payload_example: Tab (\t), form feed (\f), carriage return (\r) in commit message
attack_vectors.impact: Invalid JSON; Discord API rejects malformed payload

evidence.issue: No JSON encoding of variables; bash expansion happens before JSON validation

business_impact.notifications_fail: Discord webhook calls fail silently, missing critical heartbeat alerts
business_impact.data_exfiltration_risk: Low — but attacker could inject fields that appear as legit Discord embeds
business_impact.trust: Users relying on Discord notifications for system status cannot trust alerts if attacker commits malicious messages

fix_design.fix_strategy: Use jq command-line JSON processor with --arg parameters to safely encode all string variables
fix_design.rationale: jq --arg automatically escapes special characters (quotes, backslashes, newlines, etc.) when building JSON. No manual escaping needed.

fixed_implementation.approach: Replace heredoc JSON construction with jq -n (null input) + --arg for each user-controlled variable

pseudocode.step_1_collect_variables: BEAT_NUMBER, STATUS, MESSAGE, timestamp, commit_hash, commit_url, commit_msg, color_code, emoji
pseudocode.step_2_escape_via_jq: Pass each variable as --arg NAME "$value" to jq
pseudocode.step_3_construct_json_in_jq: Use jq filter syntax to build JSON object with interpolation: --arg x "$VAR" '{field: $x}'
pseudocode.step_4_validate_output: Output is guaranteed valid JSON; no further escaping needed

implementation_code.after_secure:
payload=$(jq -n --arg emoji "$emoji" \
                 --arg beat "$BEAT_NUMBER" \
                 --arg status "$STATUS" \
                 --arg msg "$MESSAGE" \
                 --arg ts "$timestamp" \
                 --arg hash "$commit_hash" \
                 --arg url "$commit_url" \
                 --arg commit_msg "$commit_msg" \
                 --arg color "$color_code" \
'{content: "\($emoji) **Heartbeat #\($beat)** - \($status)",embeds: [{title: "Jit Heartbeat #\($beat)", description: $msg, color: ($color | tonumber), fields: [{name: "Time", value: $ts, inline: true}, {name: "Status", value: $status, inline: true}, {name: "Latest Commit", value: "[`\($hash)`](\($url))", inline: false}, {name: "Commit Message", value: $commit_msg, inline: false}], footer: {text: "Jit Agent System", icon_url: "https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/123456789?s=32"}}]}')

files_modified.change_type: Replace vulnerable heredoc JSON construction with jq-based safe encoding

dependencies.fallback: None needed; jq is standard on all modern CI/CD environments

test_cases.name: quotes
test_cases.description: Verify double and single quotes in message are escaped
test_cases.expected: Valid JSON; quotes escaped as \" in JSON string

test_cases.name: newlines
test_cases.description: Verify literal newlines are escaped to \n

test_cases.name: backslashes
test_cases.description: Verify Windows paths with backslashes are escaped

test_cases.name: tabs
test_cases.description: Verify tab characters are escaped to \t

test_cases.name: unicode
test_cases.description: Verify Unicode (Thai, CJK, emoji) is preserved in JSON
test_cases.expected: Valid JSON; Unicode encoded as \uXXXX or preserved as UTF-8

test_cases.name: mixed
test_cases.description: Verify combination of quotes, newlines, and tabs
test_cases.expected: Valid JSON; all special chars properly escaped

test_cases.name: json_injection
test_cases.description: Verify JSON injection payload is neutralized
test_cases.expected: Payload treated as literal string, not parsed as JSON

test_cases.name: html_like
test_cases.description: Verify HTML/script-like content is treated as string, not interpreted
test_cases.expected: Valid JSON; HTML treated as literal string value

test_execution.coverage: All critical JSON injection vectors covered

test_strategy.approach: Recreate jq payload construction from discord-webhook.sh in isolated test environment
test_strategy.validation: Parse output as JSON; verify content is preserved and no injection occurred
test_strategy.edge_cases: Control characters, non-ASCII Unicode, embedded JSON, HTML entities

checklist: ✅ JSON payload output is valid (parseable by json.loads in Python)
✅ Message content preserved exactly as input (no truncation/modification)
✅ Quotes, newlines, backslashes, tabs all escaped correctly
✅ JSON injection attempts neutralized (malicious payload is treated as literal string)
✅ No regression in Discord webhook functionality

manual_verification.step_2: Run: bash scripts/discord-webhook.sh 999 ok 'Test message with "quotes" and \n newlines'
manual_verification.step_3: Verify: No JSON errors in output; Discord notification sends successfully

summary.vulnerability_brief: Unescaped variable interpolation in JSON heredoc allowed JSON injection via commit messages, newlines, quotes, and special characters.
summary.fix_brief: Replaced heredoc JSON construction with jq --arg safe encoding. All 8 injection vectors now neutralized.
summary.confidence: HIGH — jq is industry-standard JSON tool; --arg parameters are guaranteed safe.
summary.risk_post_fix: NONE — JSON is always valid and injection-safe
